questions.question: Why is TCP unsuitable for real-time media streaming?
options: TCP is too slow
TCP's retransmission adds latency and its congestion control oscillates aggressively (halving on loss), both of which break real-time communication
TCP does not support UDP
TCP requires encryption
questions.explanation: TCP retransmits lost packets, adding delay that breaks real-time media. Its congestion control halves cwnd on every loss, causing rate oscillation visible to users as quality drops.

questions.question: What is the TFRC throughput equation and what inputs does it require?
options: X = bandwidth * delay
X = s / (R * sqrt(2*p/3) + t_RTO * sqrt(3*p/8) * (1 + 32*p^3)), requiring packet size (s), RTT (R), loss rate (p), and RTO (t_RTO)
X = cwnd / RTT
X = MTU * window_size
questions.explanation: The TFRC equation predicts TCP's throughput from packet size, RTT, and loss rate. The sender plugs in these values to compute the fair sending rate without TCP's aggressive oscillation.

questions.question: How does TFRC adapt its sending rate differently from TCP Reno?
options: TFRC halves the rate on every loss, just like TCP
TFRC decreases by at most 50% per RTT and increases gradually, producing a smoother rate trajectory than TCP's sawtooth
TFRC never decreases its rate
TFRC doubles its rate on every ack
questions.explanation: TFRC limits rate decrease to at most 50% per RTT and increases gradually (not doubling). This produces a smooth sending rate suitable for media, while maintaining long-term fairness to TCP.

questions.question: What does the Jain fairness index measure, and what value indicates perfect fairness?
options: It measures throughput; higher is better
J = (sum(xi))^2 / (n * sum(xi^2)); J=1 means all flows have equal throughput (perfect fairness)
It measures packet loss; lower is better
It measures latency; lower is better
questions.explanation: The Jain fairness index ranges from 1/n (worst) to 1 (best). J=1 means all flows share bandwidth equally. TFRC and TCP coexisting should achieve J > 0.9.

questions.question: Why is unregulated UDP problematic for the network?
options: It is too secure
It has no congestion control, so it floods the network and starves TCP flows (Jain index < 0.3), causing congestion collapse
It uses too much CPU
It requires a special license
questions.explanation: Unregulated UDP has no congestion control. It sends at a fixed rate regardless of network conditions, consuming all available bandwidth and starving TCP flows that back off on loss. TFRC fixes this by adding TCP-friendly rate control.
